35 - Youre Building Rapport All Wrong (Do This Instead!) - YouTube.mp4
Quit the small talk because rapport is not built by being liked. It's built by being trusted. There's a big difference because people do not buy from people. They buy from people who they trust can get them the best result. If they like you, that's just a bonus, but they don't buy from you just because they like you. They buy from you only. Now I'm going to show you three things that you've got to master. If you want to be a top 1% salesperson and build rapport. Okay. Number one, Most rapport building is actually built by your tonality. So write this down. This is a writer downer. Your tone is how your prospect interprets. intention behind everything you say and everything you ask. Okay. So I'll give you an example.
Let's says, oh, you know, I just, you know, I'm looking at life insurance because, you know, my brother died about two months ago. What would most salespeople do in that space? They say something like. Oh, I'm so sorry to hear that. Now, let me ask you. And then they just go into the next question. How much report did you build there? Zero. In fact, you probably lost the deal because they feel like you don't freaking. care. But if I just leaned in when they said something like that and I said, oh, what actually happened? You sound so young. Your brother must have been young. What actually happened? Oh, he had this problem. He had this problem. Oh, my gosh, I'm so sorry to hear that. Now, how much life insurance did he leave his wife?
Okay. And I see that tone. That's a concern tone. Oh, when I... happened? You sound so young. What actually happened to him? Now notice I'm saying that in a concerned tone, a tone that shows empathy. What if I said it like this? Oh, what actually happened? That tone doesn't do anything. They're like, well, this is kind of private. But I'm like, oh, what actually happened? And notice what I did. I put my hand on my chest. That's a ball. language signal that shows what that you're concerned for them even if you're selling on the phone that right there right your body language affects your tonality okay so right here you kind of go into like, I'm concerned. So you go into that, your tone shifts and sometimes you can be like, oh, what actually happened?
That's just a little example about how to build rapport. Okay. Remember your tone is how the prospect interprets your intention. Bind everything you say and you ask. So in another example, let's say if I sold some type of SaaS product that automates something for a company, instead of having to manually do it, taking up hours or time that other projects don't get done that make them revenue. I might come in and say, after I found out that they're doing something, I'm like, 'Oh, how? How long have they been forcing you to do it manually for?' See how my tone shifts there? How long have they been forcing you to have to do it manually though? Notice it said 'forcing you.' Nobody likes to be forced, but notice.
how I say it in a concerned tone, a tone that shows empathy. Now, does that mean I'm going to ask every question in a concerned tone? No, it wouldn't make any sense. There's other questions I need to ask more of a curious. tone. Oh, walk me through. How do you guys generate new leads and clients? Just so I understand— if I sold for a marketing agency, that's a curious tone. I'm genuinely curious. Okay. The point is your tone is most of the sale, how you come across, how you ask the questions. Reason number two leads into what I just said. Don't ask predictable questions that every salesperson asks. Okay. So if you ask questions where the prospect knows where that question leads, like if you're trying to find problems and you say, 'Can you tell me two problems that keep you awake at night?' or 'Can you tell me some challenges you're having?' Or can you tell me some issues that's going on?
That is such a predictable question. Every salesperson is asking that, 'That sells anything.' And because it's such a predictable question, your prospect, do they know where that question probably leads? Yes. And that's why you notice if they're a laydown sale, they open up. If they're not, you notice they kind of give you vague, generalized, surface-level answers. Are you with me? So I got to reframe the questions. Okay. So let's say, if I sell for a marketing agency, as an example, and I find out the prospect is already using a different marketing agency. I might say, and this is not my first question, but probably, I don't know— eight, 10 minutes in, I might say, so you've already been using XYZ agency the last five years. I mean, they're fairly decent.
I mean, what's caused you to feel like... You might want to look at someone else or what's caused you to feel like they're not really going to be able to scale you if that's what they said they wanted. OK, notice how I reframe the question, not what. What are some problems you don't like about the marketing agency? Because they know where that leads. Now I'm getting them to push back and defend themselves on why they don't like the other marketing agency, which of course is what you want. That helps them. I mean, you're already with XYZ. You've already been doing this internally the last two years. I mean, what's causing you to feel like... your internal people might not be able to really grow the business where you want.
Well, we like them, but, and then they start telling you what they don't like, what they want to change. Okay. That's just an example of reframing the question. I'm interrupting the pattern. The prospect brain does not understand. And I'm getting them to focus and defend themselves on why they want to change, which is probably what you want, right? Hey guys, Jeremy Miner here. And number three, you've got to use more neutral language, especially in the beginning parts of the conversation. Most salespeople try to assume the sale way too early in the conversation. And because you try to assume it way too early, especially if they're an A-type, you're going to notice what they do. I didn't say I was ready to buy yet, but we're still looking around. Yeah, John, I just called. I saw your inquiry about, you know, being interested in our XYZ product. Well, I'm just kind of looking at my different options. See how you assume that. But if I simply made it more neutral, John, I got your inquiry. You asked us to give you a call back about possibly looking at ways to grow your business, right? Possibly. Looking at ways.
Notice possibly is a neutral word. I'm not assumptive there. Now, do I know I can help them? Yes. Does the prospect know I can help them? The first 30 seconds to a few minutes of a call? No. Or a conversation? No. Okay. Because I don't, how much trust and credibility do I have? Not much. I have to build that by building the gap, okay? So I'm going to use more neutral language like, what would you possibly be looking for? Or what would you might want to look at? Or what would you maybe want to go through if I did have time? See, I'm using that maybe, possibly. might be, we might be able to help you. We just have to look, you know, we might be able to do something for it, but I need to know a little bit more.
